content.text: WSGI (Web Server Gateway Interface) and ASGI (Asynchronous Server Gateway Interface) are the two foundational Python standards that define how web application code communicates with web servers. Every Python web application — whether a simple Flask endpoint or a complex multi-agent FastAPI service — depends on one of these interfaces. Choosing the right one shapes your application's concurrency model, streaming capabilities, and deployment strategy.
WSGI is defined in PEP 3333 (originally PEP 333) and describes a synchronous callable interface: a web server calls your Python application as
is a dictionary containing all request information and

is a callable used to begin the HTTP response. The application returns an iterable of byte strings that form the response body. This synchronous, blocking model was designed in 2003 and has powered Flask, Django, Pyramid, and most Python web frameworks for over two decades.
ASGI is defined in the ASGI specification (maintained by the Django project) and describes an asynchronous callable interface: the server calls your application as a coroutine
is a dictionary describing the connection type (HTTP, WebSocket, or lifespan),
is an awaitable that yields incoming messages, and

is an awaitable that accepts outgoing messages. This asynchronous model enables a single worker to handle thousands of concurrent connections, streaming responses, and persistent WebSocket connections without spawning a new thread per connection.

The critical difference is not just syntax — it is the entire execution model. WSGI applications are called once per request and block the worker until they return. ASGI applications participate in an event loop, pausing at each

point to allow other coroutines to progress. For agentic AI services that spend most of their time waiting for LLM API responses, database reads, or tool HTTP calls, this distinction can mean the difference between needing 100 worker processes and needing one worker with 1000 concurrent coroutines.

attrs.alt: WSGI vs ASGI request flow comparison

content.text: Modern agentic AI applications have a fundamentally different I/O profile compared to traditional web applications. A user request to an AI agent might trigger five tool calls in parallel (web search, code execution, file read, database query, external API), then await a 30-second LLM inference, then stream 500 tokens back to the user token-by-token. This pattern exposes the cost of WSGI's blocking model with brutal clarity.

Under WSGI, a Gunicorn worker processing that 30-second LLM call holds a process-level OS thread blocked in a network wait. If you have 4 workers and 4 simultaneous users, the fifth user queues. Under ASGI, a Uvicorn worker running the equivalent async coroutine suspends at
and immediately serves other requests. The event loop switches between hundreds of suspended coroutines with microsecond overhead, and no OS thread is blocked.

Beyond concurrency, WSGI simply cannot natively support WebSocket connections or true server-sent event streaming. WSGI does allow chunked transfer encoding for approximate streaming, but the semantics are limited and server support varies. ASGI was designed from the ground up for HTTP/1.1 streaming, HTTP/2 server push, WebSocket bidirectional messaging, and long-lived persistent connections — all of which are increasingly important for real-time AI agents.
The choice also matters for lifespan management. ASGI defines a

scope that fires startup and shutdown events at the application level, enabling you to open connection pools, load model weights, or start background tasks when the server boots and cleanly close them when it shuts down. WSGI has no equivalent standardised mechanism.
the mental model is a plain Python function call — easy to debug, trace, and reason about
20+ years of production deployments, known failure modes, extensive documentation
Gunicorn, uWSGI, mod_wsgi all have decades of operational knowledge
each Gunicorn worker is an independent OS process — a crash in one does not affect others
no need to think about event loops, coroutine scheduling, or async context managers
virtually every Python library is synchronous and works without wrappers
a single Uvicorn worker can handle thousands of concurrent I/O-bound requests
bidirectional, persistent connections are first-class citizens in the scope model
server-sent events and chunked HTTP streaming work natively without workarounds
standardised startup/shutdown hooks for connection pools and shared resources
ASGI servers (Hypercorn) support multiplexed streams natively
for I/O-heavy workloads, ASGI uses far fewer OS threads and less memory
Neither interface is universally superior. The right choice depends on your workload characteristics and team expertise.
the easy path for simple agents. Works fine at low concurrency. Breaks under load when LLM call latency is 10-30s and users pile up.
optimal for concurrent agent workloads but requires every library in the call stack to be async-compatible (SQLAlchemy async, httpx, asyncpg, etc.).
the danger zone. Calling a blocking library like psycopg2 or the Anthropic SDK's sync client directly inside an async handler blocks the entire event loop for all requests. Fix with
mounting a WSGI sub-app inside an ASGI app (via a3 or similar) adds complexity and context-switching overhead that can cancel out ASGI's benefits.
ASGI async stack traces are harder to interpret — exceptions surface across
boundaries, and race conditions in coroutines are subtle. Tools like
ASGI applications with lifespan startup (loading embedding models, opening DB pools) have longer cold-start times than stateless WSGI apps.
The WSGI specification defines the minimal interface contract. A WSGI application is any callable that accepts two arguments and returns an iterable. The server passes the request in
(method, path, headers, body as a file-like object) and a

# Minimal WSGI application (no framework)
def application(environ, start_response):
    method = environ["REQUEST_METHOD"]
    path = environ["PATH_INFO"]
    
    status = "200 OK"
    headers = [("Content-Type", "application/json; charset=utf-8")]
    start_response(status, headers)
    
    import json
    body = json.dumps({"method": method, "path": path}).encode()
    return [body]

# Run with: gunicorn myapp:application --workers 4
An ASGI application is an async callable. The
yields messages from the client (request body chunks, WebSocket frames);
dispatches response messages back to the server.
When you must call a synchronous library inside an async handler, use
(Python 3.9+) to run it in a thread pool without blocking the event loop:

import asyncio
from fastapi import FastAPI

app = FastAPI()

def sync_heavy_operation(query: str) -> str:
    # Blocking call — e.g. old sync SDK, CPU-bound embedding, etc.
    import time
    time.sleep(2)  # simulate blocking
    return f"result for {query}"

@app.get("/sync-offload")
async def run_sync_safely(q: str):
    # Run in thread pool — event loop stays free for other requests
    result = await asyncio.to_thread(sync_heavy_operation, q)
    return {"result": result}

Both interfaces sit between the HTTP server (which speaks TCP/TLS) and your application code. The server is responsible for accepting connections, parsing HTTP framing, and calling your application callable. Your application is responsible for business logic, and returns (WSGI) or sends (ASGI) the response. This clean separation is why you can swap servers (Gunicorn → uWSGI, Uvicorn → Hypercorn) without changing application code.

In production, an nginx reverse proxy typically sits in front of both WSGI and ASGI servers. Nginx handles TLS termination, static file serving, connection limiting, and request buffering. The WSGI/ASGI server handles Python application logic. This two-tier architecture allows each component to be scaled and replaced independently.
You are building a simple agent trigger endpoint with low concurrent load (< 10 simultaneous users)
You are extending an existing Flask/Django application with LLM capabilities
Your team has strong Flask expertise and the migration cost to ASGI outweighs the concurrency benefit
Your agent runs as a background Celery task and the web layer is only a simple job-submission endpoint
You are wrapping a sync-only SDK with no async equivalent and cannot afford the to_thread overhead
You are building a new agent API from scratch — the default should now be ASGI
You need to stream LLM tokens to users in real time via SSE or WebSocket
Your agent performs multiple concurrent tool calls (fan-out HTTP requests, parallel DB queries)
You expect more than 10-20 simultaneous long-running agent sessions
You need WebSocket connections for real-time bidirectional agent communication
You want clean lifespan startup hooks to initialise embedding models, vector DB connections, or thread pools
Setting up WSGI with Flask and Gunicorn

# app.py (WSGI Flask app)
from flask import Flask, jsonify, request

app = Flask(__name__)

@app.route("/agent/run", methods=["POST"])
def run_agent():
    data = request.get_json()
    query = data.get("query", "")
    # synchronous LLM call — blocks this worker
    import anthropic
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    message = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": query}]
    )
    return jsonify({"result": message.content[0].text})

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app.run()

# Deployment: gunicorn app:app --workers 4 --bind 0.0.0.0:8000
Setting up ASGI with FastAPI and Uvicorn

# app.py (ASGI FastAPI app)
from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse
from pydantic import BaseModel
import anthropic
import asyncio

app = FastAPI()
client = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic()  # async client for ASGI

class AgentRequest(BaseModel):
    query: str

@app.post("/agent/run")
async def run_agent(req: AgentRequest):
    # async LLM call — event loop stays free during await
    message = await client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": req.query}]
    )
    return {"result": message.content[0].text}

@app.post("/agent/stream")
async def stream_agent(req: AgentRequest):
    async def generate():
        async with client.messages.stream(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=1024,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": req.query}]
        ) as stream:
            async for text in stream.text_stream:
                yield f"data: {text}\n\n"
    
    return StreamingResponse(generate(), media_type="text/event-stream")

# Deployment: uvicorn app:app --workers 1 --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000
The following example demonstrates a complete ASGI lifespan pattern that initialises a shared async HTTP client on startup and cleans it up on shutdown, avoiding connection pool leaks:

from contextlib import asynccontextmanager
from fastapi import FastAPI
import httpx

# Module-level placeholder for shared client
http_client: httpx.AsyncClient | None = None

@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app: FastAPI):
    # Startup: create shared connection pool
    global http_client
    http_client = httpx.AsyncClient(
        timeout=30.0,
        limits=httpx.Limits(max_connections=100, max_keepalive_connections=20),
    )
    print("HTTP client pool initialised")
    
    yield  # application runs
    
    # Shutdown: clean up resources
    await http_client.aclose()
    print("HTTP client pool closed")

app = FastAPI(lifespan=lifespan)

@app.get("/fetch-tool")
async def fetch_tool(url: str):
    # Uses shared connection pool — no per-request overhead
    response = await http_client.get(url)
    return {"status": response.status_code, "length": len(response.content)}

A startup built an AI coding assistant as a Flask + Gunicorn application with 8 workers. Each code-review request triggered three parallel LLM calls (style check, security check, logic check), each taking an average of 8 seconds. With 8 workers and requests taking 8 seconds, the theoretical maximum throughput was 1 request per second. During a product launch, traffic hit 15 concurrent requests and latency spiked to 90 seconds as requests queued behind blocked workers.
The team migrated to FastAPI + Uvicorn over a weekend. They replaced

to issue three LLM calls in parallel. With 1 Uvicorn worker running the async code, the same 15 concurrent requests completed in 8 seconds each (limited only by LLM latency, not worker contention). Peak memory dropped from 2 GB (8 process workers) to 180 MB (single process, async).
Blocking the event loop with sync SDKs:
(sync) inside an async FastAPI route blocks every concurrent request. Always use
Assuming more Gunicorn workers fixes LLM latency:
adding workers adds memory and process overhead. If each worker spends 30s blocked on an LLM call, 4 workers still only handle 4 concurrent users. ASGI is the correct solution.
Using ASGI for CPU-bound work without process pools:
Python's GIL means one event loop core maxes out at 100% on CPU-intensive tasks like tokenisation or embedding. Use
Mixing blocking DB drivers in async code:
psycopg2 (sync) blocks the event loop. Use asyncpg or SQLAlchemy async extension. This is a common oversight when migrating from WSGI.
opening a new DB or HTTP connection per request in ASGI is wasteful. Use the lifespan pattern to create shared pools.
Deploying ASGI with multiple workers naively:
with Uvicorn, application state (like in-memory dictionaries) is not shared between workers. Use Redis or a database for shared state.
Default new AI agent APIs to ASGI (FastAPI + Uvicorn). The async ecosystem is mature enough that WSGI offers no advantage for new projects.
Always use async-native libraries: httpx instead of requests, asyncpg/SQLAlchemy async instead of psycopg2, aioredis instead of redis-py sync.
Use the ASGI lifespan pattern for every shared resource — connection pools, embedding models, background queues — to ensure clean initialisation and shutdown.
Profile before optimising — measure event loop latency with
debug mode before assuming ASGI is the bottleneck.
Keep CPU-bound operations out of the event loop — use
with Uvicorn in development for simpler debugging. Scale by running multiple single-worker containers behind a load balancer rather than multi-process single containers.

Performance characteristics differ fundamentally between WSGI and ASGI because they solve different bottlenecks. WSGI performance is bounded by the number of OS processes/threads times the request rate each can sustain. ASGI performance is bounded by the CPU available to the event loop and the number of concurrent I/O operations that can be in-flight.

For I/O-bound agentic workloads (LLM calls, tool HTTP requests, database queries), ASGI can achieve 10-100x higher throughput per unit of memory compared to WSGI. A single Uvicorn worker (Python process) can sustain 500-2000 concurrent slow I/O-bound requests with minimal memory overhead. Achieving the same with Gunicorn would require 500-2000 OS threads, consuming gigabytes of memory and exhausting the OS thread limit.

For CPU-bound workloads (embedding generation, reranking, tokenisation), ASGI provides no concurrency benefit over WSGI because Python's GIL prevents true parallel CPU execution in a single process. Horizontal scaling (multiple containers) is required for both interfaces.

Latency under ASGI is typically lower for concurrent workloads because coroutine context switching (microseconds) is much cheaper than OS thread scheduling (microseconds to milliseconds with scheduling jitter). However, ASGI adds a small fixed overhead per request for the async machinery, making WSGI marginally faster for very simple, sequential, low-concurrency endpoints.
the most popular ASGI server, built on uvloop (a libuv-based asyncio event loop). Single-worker production mode; multi-worker with
or via Gunicorn with uvicorn worker class.
ASGI server supporting HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, and HTTP/3 (QUIC). Use when HTTP/2 multiplexing or HTTP/3 is required.
ASGI server from the Django Channels project. Production-proven but less commonly used for new projects.
the standard WSGI production server. Can run Uvicorn workers via
for ASGI in a supervisor-managed multi-process setup.
feature-rich WSGI server with emperor mode (process supervisor), caching, and static file serving. Best for legacy Flask/Django deployments.

lightweight ASGI toolkit and micro-framework that FastAPI is built on. Use Starlette directly for non-API agentic services that need WebSocket support without the overhead of FastAPI's OpenAPI machinery.
Starlette ships reusable ASGI middleware for CORS, session cookies, GZip compression, HTTPS redirect, and trusted host enforcement — all composable without being tied to FastAPI.
Agent APIs, real-time AI, WebSocket apps
Both WSGI and ASGI applications inherit HTTP security concerns, but ASGI introduces some unique reliability considerations:
ASGI servers support request timeouts, but you must configure them explicitly. An LLM call that hangs indefinitely can tie up a coroutine slot. Use
ASGI lifespan shutdown events allow in-flight requests to complete before the process exits. Configure your Kubernetes
to be longer than your longest expected request duration.
that are not awaited or cancelled can accumulate. Use task groups or structured concurrency to track all spawned tasks.
both WSGI and ASGI apps behind an nginx reverse proxy need CORS headers for browser clients. Use
implement rate limiting at the ASGI middleware layer using
for FastAPI or at the nginx layer. Avoid per-request rate limit checks that hit Redis synchronously in async code.
without validation. ASGI exposes raw HTTP values; frameworks add validation on top.

Think of WSGI as a phone support center with fixed-line agents. Each call (request) occupies an agent (thread/process) for its entire duration. If 4 agents are on calls and a 5th caller rings, they wait in a queue. The system is simple to manage — you know exactly how many callers are being served — but scales only by hiring more agents.
ASGI is an air traffic controller. One controller (event loop) manages hundreds of planes (coroutines) simultaneously. The controller never flies the plane — they issue instructions (

calls) and a plane handles its own navigation while the controller turns attention to the next plane. When a plane needs clearance (I/O response), the controller parks it briefly and handles others. This scales to thousands of planes with one controller, as long as the controller is never blocked doing the flying themselves.
WSGI is a synchronous Python web interface; ASGI is its asynchronous successor enabling coroutines, WebSockets, and streaming.
For agentic AI services with high I/O concurrency (LLM calls, tool HTTP), ASGI provides dramatically higher throughput per unit of memory.
The most dangerous mistake is calling blocking I/O inside an async ASGI handler — it freezes the event loop for all concurrent requests.
Always use async-native libraries in ASGI: AsyncAnthropic, httpx, asyncpg, aioredis.
Use the ASGI lifespan pattern to manage shared resources like connection pools and loaded models.
When you must call a synchronous library, use
to offload it to a thread pool.
Deploy ASGI with Uvicorn (or Gunicorn + UvicornWorker for process supervision); deploy WSGI with Gunicorn or uWSGI.
For CPU-bound workloads, neither interface helps — use ProcessPoolExecutor or horizontal scaling.
